text: The surgeon provides a view of the anatomy, highlighting the pink stomach. The stomach is pushed against the defect where the diaphragm has separated around the esophagus, allowing the stomach to slide into the chest. Using a heat dividing instrument, the surgeon divides attachments from the diaphragm to the stomach, working to free the esophagus from the diaphragm. Attachments from the liver to the stomach are also divided, carefully sparing a small nerve in the bridge below. Once the tissue is freed, the defect will be closed, and the fundoplication wrap will be performed.

The surgeon lifts the esophagus, revealing the pink tissue below, and identifies the diaphragm encircling the esophagus. The section is carried out to close the diaphragm below the esophagus at the V-shaped defect. Non-absorbable sutures with felt pledges are placed to close the diaphragm and buttress the closure, preventing the stomach from sliding into the chest.

An extra suture is placed to secure the esophagus, with visible scarring expected. The closure below the diaphragm and esophagus is checked for adequate swallowing space. The stomach is freed from the left side near the spleen, with purple blood vessels divided using a harmonic scalpel. The stomach is brought around to create the wrap, sewn together over a rubber stent to ensure it remains open. The completed wrap is shown, with additional sutures placed to prevent sliding into the chest, completing the procedure.
